standardization_map.colloquial: velocity is 0 and the sine is changing
standardization_map.standard_term: Point of Zero Velocity / Direction Change

teaching_sequence: Point of Zero Velocity / Direction Change
Calculating Total Distance with Changing Velocity Direction

concepts.name: Displacement
concepts.description: The shortest distance from the initial to the final position of an object, including direction.

concepts.name: Distance
concepts.description: The total path length covered by an object irrespective of direction.

concepts.name: Second Equation of Motion (s = ut + 0.5at^2)
concepts.description: An equation relating initial velocity, time, acceleration, and displacement for an object under constant acceleration, used here to prompt the discussion on 's' as displacement vs. distance.

concepts.name: Velocity Function
concepts.description: A mathematical expression that describes an object's velocity as a function of time.

concepts.name: Calculating Displacement using Integration
concepts.description: Determining the net displacement of an object by integrating its velocity function with respect to time over a given interval.

concepts.name: Definite Integral
concepts.description: An integral with upper and lower limits of integration, used to find the exact value of a quantity over a specified interval.

concepts.name: Power Rule of Integration
concepts.description: A fundamental rule for integrating power functions, stating that the integral of x^n is (x^(n+1))/(n+1) + C.

concepts.name: Point of Zero Velocity / Direction Change
concepts.description: An instant in time when an object's velocity momentarily becomes zero, often indicating a reversal in its direction of motion.

concepts.name: Properties of Distance
concepts.description: Characteristics of distance, such as being a scalar quantity, always non-negative, and representing the total path length.

concepts.name: Calculating Total Distance with Changing Velocity Direction
concepts.description: A technique to find total distance by splitting the integration interval at points where velocity is zero, and summing the absolute values of displacement over each sub-interval.

prerequisites.rationale: To calculate displacement using integration, a velocity function is strictly necessary as the input for integration.
Calculating displacement over a specific time interval inherently requires the application of a definite integral.
The specific integration example demonstrated in the lecture for velocity functions relies on the power rule of integration.
Identifying the points where velocity is zero is a critical first step for correctly applying the technique of calculating total distance when direction changes.
Understanding the properties of distance (e.g., non-negativity) provides the rationale for the specific steps in the total distance calculation technique.
The technique for calculating total distance builds upon the fundamental method of calculating displacement using integration, extending it with considerations for direction changes.

video_title: Difference Between Diaplacement and Distance || IIT JEE  MAINS AND ADVANCE CONCEPT ||
